title: Time Shifting
summary: This page presents Example 1.3 on time shifting of signals, demonstrating how an exponential function x(t) = e^(-2t) is delayed and advanced by 1 second. The mathematical description of the original signal is provided, and figures illustrate the original signal alongside its right-shifted (delayed) and left-shifted (advanced) versions. The delayed function is represented as x(t-1), corresponding to a right shift of the signal.

figures.caption: Signal x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) shown in (a), delayed by 1 second in (b), and advanced by 1 second in (c).
figures.description: The figure has three time-domain plots with horizontal axis t and vertical axis labeled by the signal value: (a) x(t), (b) x(t-1), and (c) x(t+1). In (a), the signal x(t)=e^{-2t}u(t) starts at value 1 at t=0 and decays exponentially for t>0, remaining zero for t<0 due to the unit step. Panel (b) shows the same waveform delayed right by 1 second so the jump and decay begin at t=1, while panel (c) shows it advanced left by 1 second so the jump begins at t=-1. Students should notice that delay and advance shift the entire waveform horizontally without changing its exponential shape or initial amplitude.
